summary: The ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar, during which observant Muslims perform sawm — mandatory dawn-to-sunset fasting, one of the Five Pillars of Islam. Marked by the predawn suhur meal, the sunset iftar meal that traditionally opens with dates, extra tarawih night prayers, and the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr) toward its end. Concluded by the festival of Eid al-Fitr. Practices and regional customs vary widely, from Egyptian fanoos lanterns and cannon-firing to Indonesian mudik homecomings and Ramadan television serials (musalsalat).

works.name: Sawm (Fasting)
works.description: The obligatory fast, one of the Five Pillars of Islam, requiring abstention from food, drink, and sex from dawn until sunset throughout the month. Its daily start is fixed, per the Cow Surah (Al-Baqarah), as the moment a white thread can be distinguished from a black one. Hadiths promise fasters a special gate to Paradise, Ar-Rayyan, and describe fasting as a "shield" that multiplies good deeds tenfold; missed days can be atoned for by feeding sixty impoverished people.

clues.clue: Mandatory dawn-to-sunset abstention from food, drink, and sex; one of the Five Pillars, called sawm
examples: The Islamic pillar of sawm requires fasting during -what month?
Name the lunar month during which Muslims are required to abstain from eating, drinking, or having sex from sunrise until sunset.

clues.clue: The daily fast begins when a white thread can be distinguished from a black thread, per a verse in the Cow Surah (Al-Baqarah)
examples: As related in the Cow Surah, a part of this observance begins when the white and black threads are distinguishable.
The main part of this observance begins when one can tell apart white and black threads.

clues.clue: Per hadith, a faster who avoids "forged speech" and "evil actions" may cross a special gate to Paradise called Ar-Rayyan
examples: Per one hadith, God does not accept this action unless its performer gives up "forged speech" and "evil actions." People who perform this action will be able to cross a gate called Ar-Raiyan on the Day of Judgment.

clues.clue: Per another hadith, fasting serves as a "shield" and multiplies good deeds tenfold
examples: Per another hadith, this action serves as a "shield" and multiplies good deeds by ten times.

clues.clue: Intentionally missing a day of fasting can be atoned for by feeding sixty impoverished people
examples: Intentional failure to perform this action can be made up for by taking care of 60 impoverished people.

clues.clue: A weak hadith holds that Adam fasted on the "White Days" (the 13th-15th of each month) after his skin turned black
examples: A weak hadith states that, after Adam's skin turned black, he performed this action on the "White Days," or the 13th, 14th, and 15th days of each month.

clues.clue: Modern fatwas hold that COVID-19 vaccines and Pap smears do not break the fast; violators may make fidya or kaffara donations
examples: A 2021 post by Darul Iftaa claims that, unlike Pap smears, COVID-19 vaccines do not violate this practice. Muslims may make fidya or kaffara donations online if they violate this practice.

clues.clue: Tradition holds that during the month the gates of Heaven are opened, the gates of Hell are shut, and demons are chained up
examples: Supposedly, during this observance, demons are chained up and the gates of Hell are locked.
According to one source, on this holiday "the Gates of Heaven are thrown open and the Gates of Hell are shut."

works.name: Iftar
works.description: The evening meal that breaks the daily fast at sunset, coinciding with the Maghrib prayer and traditionally opened by eating dates. Regional iftar dishes are extremely varied, including South Asian nombu kanji porridge, Turkish meat-and-cheese platters, the milk-soaked pastry gullac, Hyderabadi haleem stew, and the apricot drink Qamr ad-Din.

clues.clue: The evening meal breaking the fast at sunset, taken at the same time as the Maghrib prayer, roughly twelve hours after suhur
examples: Suhoor takes place approximately twelve hours prior to these events.
Three dates are often eaten to begin these events, which take place at the same time as the after-sunset Maghrib prayers.

clues.clue: Traditionally begun by eating dates
examples: Three dates are traditionally eaten to start one meal during this observance.

clues.clue: In South India, mung beans/lentils, rice, and coconut milk are made into a porridge called nombu kanji for this meal
examples: For these events, mung beans or lentils, rice, and coconut milk are made into a porridge called nombu kanji.

clues.clue: Turkish platters for this meal usually include cheese, dried meat, and a sesame-topped pita
examples: Platters named for these events in Turkey usually include cheese and dried meat as well as a kind of pita with sesame seeds and a scored top.

clues.clue: A baklava-like dessert of thin dough soaked in sweetened milk, gullac, is typically eaten for this meal
examples: A baklava-like dessert with thin sheets of dough soaked in sweetened milk called gullac is usually eaten during this event.

clues.clue: In Hyderabad, a stew of pounded wheat, lentils, and goat meat called haleem is typically eaten for this meal
examples: A stew typically made from a thick paste of pounded wheat, lentils, and goat meat called haleem is typically eaten during these events in Hyderabad.

clues.clue: An apricot drink called Qamr ad-Din is traditionally consumed with this meal
examples: During Ramadan, this large meal usually breaks the fast during the evening. An apricot drink called Qamr ad-Din is traditionally consumed with this meal.

works.name: Suhur and the Mesaharati
works.description: Suhur (also suhoor/sahur) is the predawn meal eaten before the fast begins, roughly twelve hours before iftar and preceded by the nightly intention (niyyah). Traditionally, town criers called mesaharati wake sleepers for the meal by walking the streets playing drums — Bilal ibn Rabah is said to have been the first — a role filled by slit drums like the kentongan in parts of Southeast Asia.

clues.clue: The predawn meal eaten before the fast begins, roughly twelve hours before iftar, following the nightly intention (niyyah)
examples: Suhoor takes place approximately twelve hours prior to these events.
One of the religious observations during this period is the making of niyyah every night and the eating of a morning meal before fajr.

clues.clue: Town criers called mesaharati traditionally wake people for the meal by walking the streets playing drums; Bilal ibn Rabah is said to have been the first to serve in this role
examples: Observers of this occurrence employ helpers called mesaharati who are tasked with walking through the streets at night playing drums.
Possibly the first mesaharati to proclaim the start of suhur was Bilal ibn Rabah, who was also the first person to serve in this role.

clues.clue: In parts of Southeast Asia, a slit drum called the kentongan replaces the mesaharati; Egyptian mesaharati use bazah drums before the fajr prayer
examples: In parts of Southeast Asia, the kentongan slit drum replaces the use of a mesaharati during this observance.
Egyptian bazah drums are used by mesaharati to signal this event before the fajr prayer.

works.name: Tarawih
works.description: Extra, optional night prayers performed in pairs (rak'ahs) after the Isha prayer throughout the month. Many Sunni Muslims aim to complete a full recitation of the Quran (khatm) during tarawih by reading one juz per night, often led by a hafiz; the completion is sometimes dedicated to the recently deceased.

clues.clue: Optional night prayers, performed in pairs, following the Isha prayer throughout the month
examples: During this event, Muslims may follow the Isha prayer with a cycle of optional prayers called tarawih.
Many people believe that this time period requires extra nighttime prayers called Tarawih.

clues.clue: Sunni Muslims may attempt a full Quran recitation (khatm) during these prayers by reading one juz per night, led by a hafiz
examples: By reading one 'juz per night, Sunni Muslims may attempt a complete recitation of the Quran during these prayers.

During each day of Ramadan, many mosques have Taraweeh prayer under a Hafez in order to accomplish this task by the end of the month. This task is called a khatm, and is also sometimes done to help the recently deceased.

works.name: Night of Power and I'tikaf
works.description: Laylat al-Qadr, the Night of Power, falls among the odd-numbered nights of the last ten days of the month; tradition holds the Quran was first revealed to Muhammad on this night, and worship performed on it is said to be worth more than a thousand months. Devout Muslims may mark the period with i'tikaf, a retreat of secluding oneself in a mosque; signs said to mark the approaching night include a moon resembling half a plate and a "feeble and red" sunrise with no rays.

clues.clue: Falls on an odd-numbered night among the last ten days of the month; worship on it is said to be worth more than a thousand months, and it marks when the Quran was first revealed to Muhammad
examples: Worship done during this occasion towards the end of Ramadan is said to be better than a thousand months of worship. The Quran was first sent down and revealed to Muhammad during this occasion.
it includes the Night of Power or Laylat al-Qadr, which is held on an odd-numbered day.

clues.clue: Devout Muslims may seclude themselves in a mosque during this period in the practice of i'tikaf
examples: Devout Muslims may observe this event by secluding themselves in a mosque in the practice of i'tikaf.
A form of retreat known as i'tikaf is most commonly practiced during a period of time with this name during which the Night of Power occurs.

clues.clue: Signs said to mark its approach include a moon resembling half of a plate and a "feeble and red" sunrise with no rays
examples: Near the end of this observance, people look for signs such as the moon appearing like half of a plate and a "feeble and red" sunrise with no rays.

works.name: Ramadan Television (Musalsalat)
works.description: Arabic-language TV serials called musalsalat traditionally premiere during the month, largely replacing older post-iftar oral storytellers. Egyptian nightly viewership nearly doubles, driving fierce competition among new shows (140 competed for ad slots in 2017); notable examples include Harat al-Yehud, one of the first Egyptian shows to depict a sympathetic Jewish character, and Muhtesem Yuzyil, a Turkish series about Suleiman the Magnificent watched by an estimated 150 million people.

clues.clue: Arabic-language TV serials (musalsalat) traditionally premiere during the month, replacing the role of older post-iftar oral storytellers; Adel Emam is among their most famous creators
examples: In Arabic countries, television dramas called musalsal traditionally premiere during this event.

In much of the Islamic world, the role of traditional post-iftar storytellers have largely been replaced by examples of these things, which are called musalsalat. Adel Emam is one of the most famous creators of them.

clues.clue: Egyptian nightly viewership of mosalsalat nearly doubles during the month; 140 new series competed for ad slots in 2017, with Kalabsh a 2018 breakout hit
examples: During this period, Egyptian viewership of the nightly mosalsalat TV serials almost doubles. Identify this season when some 140 new television series fought for prime advertising rates in 2017. In 2018, Kalabsh was a breakout hit during this season.

clues.clue: The 2015 series Harat al-Yehud featured one of the first positive Jewish characters on Egyptian TV, Moussa Haroun
examples: The 2015 Ramadan series Harat al-Yehud featured one of the first positive depictions of a member of this religious faith on Egyptian TV in the character of Moussa Haroun, who founds a kibbutz after a 1948 war.

clues.clue: The Turkish series Muhtesem Yuzyil, depicting this ruler, was watched by an estimated 150 million people; Erdogan threatened to prosecute its producers
examples: An estimated 150 million people watched Halit Ergenc play this man on the show Muhtesem Yuzyıl. In 2012, Recep Tayyip Erdogan announced he'd prosecute the show's producers for their portrayal of this man.

works.name: Regional and Cultural Customs
works.description: Local traditions surrounding the month vary widely: Indonesians observe mudik (a mass homecoming) followed by eating ketupat rice dumplings, clean cemeteries in advance during nyadran, ritually bathe in springs during Padusan, and receive the mandatory THR bonus; Egyptians fire a cannon and hang fanoos lanterns to mark fasting times; and the historical Ottoman court set up silk swings between minarets, operated by Janissaries and paid for in akce coins, as a popular nighttime pastime. Large evening social gatherings called ghabga are also common.

clues.clue: In Indonesia, marked by mudik, a mass homecoming tradition, after which people eat ketupat rice dumplings
examples: This period is marked in some countries by a homecoming tradition known as mudik, after which people eat foods such as rice dumplings called ketupat.

clues.clue: In Indonesia, villagers clean cemeteries beforehand in the nyadran tradition, and ritually bathe in springs in the Padusan tradition
examples: Two weeks before this event, villagers clean cemeteries in a local tradition called the nyadran.
Padusan is a tradition of bathing in springs before this observance, during which lanterns called fanous are hung.

clues.clue: In Indonesia, employers must pay workers the THR bonus at least a week before the month's largest observance
examples: This is the largest observance for which employers must pay workers the THR bonus at least a week before it begins.

clues.clue: In Egypt, a cannon is fired and colorful fanoos lanterns are hung to mark the month
examples: The tradition of firing a cannon during this event began in Egypt, which marks this event with colorful fanoos lanterns.

clues.clue: Large evening social gatherings called ghabga are held during the month
examples: This event provides the occasion for large social gatherings known as ghabga.

clues.clue: In the Ottoman Empire, silk swings hung between minarets, decorated with lanterns, were a popular nighttime pastime, run by Janissaries and paid for with akce coins
examples: Swings were most popular during this period of the year during which lanterns were hung between minarets and people frequently attended lengthy prayers and lavish feasts between sunset and sunrise.
Swings were normally run by this group who were traditionally recruited by the Devshirme process and who were disbanded and killed in the Auspicious Incident.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Standard closing/identifying clues for Ramadan as the ninth month of the Islamic calendar, plus miscellaneous etymological and historical trivia about the month that did not cluster into a larger section.

clues.clue: The ninth month of the Islamic (lunar) calendar, more sacred than the preceding month of Muharram and followed by Shawwal
examples: This is the ninth month of the Islamic calendar, which emphasizes fasting.
The only month more sacred than Muharram is this month of fasting which precedes Shawwal.

clues.clue: Concluded/marked by the festival of Eid al-Fitr, celebrated at the sighting of the new moon
examples: Eid al-Fitr concludes what Islamic month of fasting?
The sighting of a new moon at the end of this month is celebrated with the festival of Eid al-Fitr.

clues.clue: Name derives from a root (ramad) meaning the "scorching heat of the ground"; the word itself appears only once in the Quran (a hapax legomenon)
examples: With a name derived from a word representing the scorching heat of the ground, it includes the reading of special prayers called Tarawih and it culminates in the Eid-ul-Fitr.
Another hapax legomenon in the Quran is this word for the ninth month of the Islamic calendar, a month of ritual fasting.

clues.clue: Its practices were syncretized with pre-Islamic Sabian customs of worshipping the moon's disappearance and reappearance
examples: The practices of this holiday were syncretized with the Sabian customs of worshipping the disappearance and then reappearance of the Moon.

clues.clue: Folklorist Margaret Hasluck noted it is commonly used as a personal name for children born during the month — a naming pattern shared by the Swiss scholar Tariq Ramadan
examples: Margaret Hasluck commented on the prevalence of this holiday being used as a last name for children born during it, and one scholar with this name wrote What I Believe and The Quest for Meaning.

This is the last name of a Swiss philosopher who was banned from entering the U.S. until 2010, a man who wrote Western Muslims and the Future of Islam and teaches Oriental Studies at Oxford University.

comprehensive_summary: Ramadan is the ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar, more sacred than the preceding month of Muharram and immediately followed by Shawwal; its name likely derives from a root, ramad, meaning the "scorching heat of the ground," and the word itself is a hapax legomenon, appearing only once in the Quran. Its defining practice is sawm, obligatory dawn-to-sunset fasting and one of the Five Pillars of Islam: the daily fast begins, per a verse in the Cow Surah, when a white thread can be distinguished from a black one, and continues until sunset, when the fast is broken with iftar. Iftar traditionally opens with dates and features enormously varied regional dishes — South Asian nombu kanji porridge, Turkish meat-and-cheese platters, the pastry gullac, Hyderabadi haleem stew, and the apricot drink Qamr ad-Din — while the predawn suhur meal, taken roughly twelve hours earlier after the nightly niyyah (intention), is traditionally announced by town-crier drummers called mesaharati (said to trace back to Bilal ibn Rabah) or, in parts of Southeast Asia, a kentongan slit drum. Hadiths promise fasters a special gate to Paradise (Ar-Rayyan) and describe fasting as a "shield" multiplying good deeds tenfold; missed fasts can be atoned for by feeding sixty impoverished people, and popular tradition holds that during the month Heaven's gates open, Hell's gates shut, and demons are chained. Nightly tarawih prayers, performed in pairs after Isha, often accompany an attempt by Sunni Muslims to complete a full Quran recitation (khatm) at a rate of one juz per night. Toward the month's end falls Laylat al-Qadr, the Night of Power, on an odd-numbered night among the last ten days — when the Quran is said to have first been revealed to Muhammad and worship is worth more than a thousand months — which devout Muslims may mark with i'tikaf, a retreat of mosque seclusion. The month is also a major cultural and media event: Arabic-language TV serials (musalsalat) traditionally premiere then, nearly doubling Egyptian nightly viewership and producing hits like Harat al-Yehud and the Turkish series Muhtesem Yuzyil. Regional customs are similarly rich — Indonesia's mass mudik homecoming, cemetery-cleaning nyadran, and spring-bathing Padusan; Egypt's fasting-signaling cannon and fanoos lanterns; and the historical Ottoman custom of silk swings hung between minarets — and the month closes with the festival of Eid al-Fitr.
recursive_suggestions: Eid al-Fitr — the concluding festival, frequently paired with Ramadan clues; deserves its own topic if not already queued
Sawm / Five Pillars of Islam — the broader pillar framework Ramadan fasting belongs to
Islam — general-identity clues about the religion appeared in this clue pool (T9) but were filtered out as off-topic; flag if not yet processed
Tariq Ramadan — a Swiss scholar whose surname is used as wordplay misdirection for this topic; distinct person, not to be conflated
Yom Kippur War / Operation Badr — coincidentally also called the "Ramadan War"; unrelated 1973 conflict, filtered out of this analysis (T5, T10, T14)
